description: Internal flow documentation for ascii-chat — how data moves through the system

flows.title: Options Parsing & RCU Publishing
flows.subtitle: How a CLI flag (or WASM call) becomes a live option readable by any thread
flows.summary: Options go through a five-stage pipeline: mode detection → registry lookup → parsing → validation → RCU atomic publish. After publishing, any thread can read via GET_OPTION() with zero locks.

stages.name: Entry Point
stages.description: main() calls options_init(argc, argv). On WASM/web, JS calls the exported C function directly, populating the same options_t struct.

files.note: Calls options_init() then options_get()
Unified entry point for all parsing

stages.name: Mode Detection
stages.description: Scans argv for the mode keyword (server, client, mirror, discovery-service) or a session string. Falls back to discovery mode if no mode given. Binary-level flags (--log-file, --verbose, etc.) are parsed here too.

files.note: Detects mode before builder runs
Maps 'server' → MODE_SERVER etc.

stages.name: Registry Lookup
stages.description: A global registry_entry_t[] table (up to 2048 slots) holds every option's metadata: long name, short name, type (bool/int/string/enum/double), field offset in options_t, env var name, range constraints, validator. Categories (audio, display, network, security, media…) are merged at startup via registry_init_from_builders().

files.note: Merges all category arrays into g_options_registry[]
Example category: port, address, fps…
Audio category entries
Color, palette, render mode…
Crypto/auth options

stages.name: Argument Parsing
stages.description: The option builder walks argv using the mode-specific preset (options_preset_unified). For each --flag, it finds the registry entry and calls the appropriate typed parser. Custom parsers handle complex types (timestamps, port ranges, palette strings, PCRE2 grep patterns, session strings).

files.note: Builder-based argv walking
Core builder logic
Typed parsers with PCRE2 validation
TOML config file merge (--config)
Mode-specific option sets

stages.name: Environment Variable Override
stages.description: After CLI parsing, each registry entry's env_var_name (e.g. ASCII_CHAT_FPS) is checked. Env vars override config-file values but are overridden by explicit CLI flags. The special ASCII_CHAT_QUESTION_PROMPT_RESPONSE stack pre-answers interactive prompts for automation.

files.note: Iterates registry, applies env vars
Cross-field validation after env merge

stages.name: RCU Publish
stages.description: options_state_init() allocates an options_t on the heap and stores its pointer in a global atomic_ptr_t (g_options). options_state_set() memcpy's the parsed struct in. Worker threads read via options_get() / GET_OPTION() which does a single atomic_ptr_load — no locks. Runtime updates (e.g. live FPS change) call options_set_int() which does a full copy-on-write: allocate new struct, memcpy, call updater, atomic swap, defer-free old.

files.note: Allocates initial options_t, stores in g_options
Lock-free atomic_ptr_load; falls back to g_default_options
Copy-on-write writer path (mutex-serialized)
Old structs freed at shutdown (up to 64 deferred)
Convenience macro: options_get()->field

notes: The static g_default_options is used as a safe fallback before options_state_init() and after options_state_destroy() — atexit handlers can call GET_OPTION() safely.
Fork safety: pthread_atfork() handler resets the lifecycle and atomic pointer in the child process.
WASM: The same options_t struct and GET_OPTION() macro are used. The JS caller sets fields via exported C wrappers before starting the render loop.

flows.title: Media Pipeline: Input → ASCII → Terminal
flows.subtitle: From webcam / file / stdin / URL all the way to colored glyphs on the screen
flows.summary: A unified media_source_t abstraction hides webcam, FFmpeg-decoded files, yt-dlp-resolved URLs, and stdin pipes behind one interface. Frames flow through SIMD-accelerated RGBA conversion, luminance-to-character mapping, ANSI color code generation, and finally write() to the terminal fd.

stages.name: Media Source Creation
stages.description: media_source_create() examines the options: if --file/-f is set it picks FILE mode (or STDIN for '-'). If --url is set, the smart resolver tries a direct FFmpeg open first, then falls back to yt-dlp subprocess to extract the real stream URL (cached 30 s). Webcam is the default. Test pattern is activated by --test-pattern.

files.note: Factory: picks WEBCAM / FILE / STDIN / TEST / URL
Detects .mp4, .mkv, .m3u8, rtsp:// etc.
Runs yt-dlp subprocess, caches result 30s
Opens AVFormatContext; AVIO buffer for stdin
V4L2 on Linux, AVFoundation on macOS

stages.name: Frame Decode
stages.description: media_source_read_video_frame() returns an image_t* (RGBA pixels). For webcam: V4L2/AVFoundation captured frame converted by SwsContext to RGBA. For files: FFmpeg reads packets, decodes via AVCodecContext, SwsContext rescales to terminal pixel dimensions. For stdin: AVIO reads from fd 0 into a 64 KB buffer.

files.note: Dispatches to webcam_capture or ffmpeg_decoder_read_video_frame
av_read_frame → avcodec_decode → sws_scale → image_t
RGBA framebuffer with SAFE_MALLOC tracking

stages.name: Color Filter / Effects
stages.description: After frame decode, optional color_filter_apply() modifies the RGBA pixels. Filters include: GRAYSCALE, SEPIA, INVERT, HIGH_CONTRAST, COOL, WARM, MATRIX_GREEN, NIGHT_VISION, CRT. These run on full RGBA data before ASCII conversion. --color-filter selects the filter; --color-scheme-name picks a named palette.

files.note: In-place RGBA pixel transformation
--matrix mode: overlays falling katakana glyphs

stages.name: RGBA → ASCII Conversion
stages.description: ascii_convert() maps each pixel block to a character using a luminance palette. The dispatcher picks the fastest SIMD path available (AVX2 > SSSE3 > SSE2 > NEON/SVE on ARM > scalar). For color mode, each cell gets ANSI SGR escape sequences (16-color, 256-color, or 24-bit truecolor depending on terminal capability). For halfblock mode (▀▄), each character represents two vertical pixels.

files.note: Main entry: dispatches to SIMD-specific paths
256-color AVX2 path (8 pixels/cycle)
ARM NEON path
Fallback scalar path
Returns char string for selected palette type
Generates ANSI SGR color escape codes

stages.name: RLE Compression (network)
stages.description: When sending frames over the network, ascii_rle_encode() compresses repeated characters (runs of same char+color). Typical compression ratio is 3-8x for video frames. The receiver calls ascii_rle_decode(). Disabled by --no-compress. Threshold: packets > 100 KB are compressed.

files.note: Run-length encoding for ASCII frame payloads
Accumulates ASCII data before sending

stages.name: Terminal Rendering
stages.description: session_display_render_frame() writes the ASCII string (with ANSI escapes) to the terminal fd. It uses cursor repositioning (\033[H) rather than clearing the screen to minimize flicker. If --strip-ansi is set, escape codes are stripped before writing. For --snapshot mode, a single frame is written to stdout and the process exits.

files.note: Repositions cursor, writes ANSI frame atomically
Detects TTY; suppresses sequences when piped
Sets raw mode, hides cursor, clears screen

notes: Audio follows a parallel path: media_source_read_audio() returns PCM floats → Opus encoder → PACKET_TYPE_AUDIO_OPUS_BATCH sent over network. Locally, PortAudio plays decoded PCM.
Stdin pipe mode: pass --file '-' or pipe data to the process. FFmpeg opens stdin via an AVIO context with a 64 KB buffer.
yt-dlp extracted URLs are cached for 30 seconds to avoid repeated subprocess spawns during startup FPS probing.
For --render-file output: the frame goes through libvterm (terminal emulation) + FreeType2 (glyph rasterization) → RGBA pixel buffer → FFmpeg H.264 encoder → output .mp4 file.

flows.title: Render-File Pipeline: ASCII → Pixel Video
flows.subtitle: How --render-file turns ASCII art into a real video file via libvterm + FreeType2 + FFmpeg
flows.summary: render_file_create() builds a chain: terminal_renderer (libvterm emulates a terminal, FreeType2 rasterizes glyphs into an RGBA pixel buffer) → ffmpeg_encoder (encodes the pixel buffer as H.264 into an output file or stdout). Each ASCII frame is fed through the chain, synchronized with audio from PortAudio or the media source.

stages.name: Context Creation
stages.description: render_file_create() resolves the font (platform_font_resolve: fontconfig on Linux, CoreText on macOS, or bundled matrix font). Creates a term_renderer_config_t specifying cols×rows cells and font size. Passes that to term_renderer_create() (libvterm + FreeType2), then to ffmpeg_encoder_create() which opens the output path (or stdout if '-').

files.note: Orchestrates font resolution, renderer, encoder creation
fontconfig / CoreText / bundled matrix font
Initializes VTerm + FreeType2 + per-renderer glyph cache
Opens output container (mp4/stdout), H.264 codec

stages.name: Frame Rendering
stages.description: render_file_push_frame() writes the ASCII string (with ANSI escapes) into libvterm via vterm_input_write(). libvterm parses the escape sequences and updates its VTermScreen cell grid. Then term_renderer_render_to_buffer() iterates each cell: looks up the Unicode codepoint in the per-renderer FreeType2 glyph cache (hash table), blends the glyph bitmap onto an RGBA framebuffer at cell_w × cell_h pixels. The result is one full terminal frame as pixels.

files.note: VTermScreen → pixel buffer via FreeType2 glyph cache
Hash lookup + FT_Load_Glyph + rasterization on miss
Calls term_renderer_render + ffmpeg_encoder_write_frame

stages.name: FFmpeg Encoding
stages.description: The RGBA pixel buffer is converted to YUV420p (via SwsContext) and submitted to the FFmpeg H.264 encoder (AVCodecContext). Encoded packets are written to the output AVFormatContext (mp4 container). If output_path is '-' or NULL, the AVIO context routes bytes to stdout, enabling piping to other ffmpeg processes.

files.note: RGBA → YUV420p → H.264 AVPacket → AVFormatContext
stdout path: avio_open with custom AVIO callback

stages.name: Audio Mixing
stages.description: Audio samples are collected from either a media_source (file audio via ffmpeg_decoder) or a live PortAudio capture ring buffer. render_file_push_audio_from_source() drains the ring buffer and submits float PCM to ffmpeg_encoder_write_audio(). The encoder converts to AAC and muxes alongside the video.

files.note: Reads audio ring buffer, submits PCM to encoder
Float PCM → AAC → mux into mp4

notes: The glyph cache is per-renderer and per-font. A cache miss triggers FT_Load_Glyph + FT_Render_Glyph and stores the bitmap. Typical cache hit rate is >99% after the first frame.
Stdout output enables: ascii-chat mirror --render-file '-' | ffmpeg -i - output.mp4 for live transcoding.
Matrix rain mode auto-selects the bundled matrix font (Courier-like monospace katakana variant) unless --render-font overrides it.

flows.title: Network Protocol: Client ↔ Server
flows.subtitle: How packets flow between client and server over TCP, WebSocket, or WebRTC
flows.summary: All communication is routed through an acip_transport_t abstraction that hides whether the underlying connection is TCP, WebSocket, or WebRTC. Packets have a fixed 24-byte header (magic, type, length, CRC32, client_id) followed by a variable payload. After the crypto handshake, all session packets are encrypted.

stages.name: Connection Establishment
stages.description: The client resolves the server address and opens a TCP socket (or WebSocket/WebRTC if configured). An acip_transport_t is created wrapping the socket with a function-pointer vtable (send/recv/is_connected/get_socket). Both ends are now ready for the handshake.

files.note: Creates and wraps TCP socket
WebSocket upgrade path
Assembles transport, initiates handshake

stages.name: Crypto Handshake
stages.description: The handshake is a 6-message exchange using X25519 ephemeral DH + optional Ed25519 identity keys. Client sends CRYPTO_CLIENT_HELLO → server responds CRYPTO_CAPABILITIES → CRYPTO_PARAMETERS → CRYPTO_KEY_EXCHANGE_INIT (server ephemeral pubkey + identity pubkey + signature) → client sends CRYPTO_KEY_EXCHANGE_RESP → both derive shared secret via X25519 → server sends CRYPTO_HANDSHAKE_COMPLETE. Password auth inserts a CRYPTO_AUTH_CHALLENGE/RESPONSE round. All subsequent packets use XSalsa20-Poly1305 AEAD.

files.note: Receives server keys, validates signature, sends client key
Sends server keys, derives shared secret
X25519 DH: crypto_box_beforenm() from libsodium
TOFU: verifies server key against ~/.ssh/known_hosts
Parses PEM/OpenSSH ed25519 keys

stages.name: Packet Structure
stages.description: Every packet: [magic:8B][type:2B][length:4B][crc32:4B][client_id:4B][payload:N]. Magic is the hex approximation of 'ASCIICCHAT'. CRC32 is computed over the payload. For encrypted packets, the payload is XSalsa20-Poly1305 ciphertext (type = PACKET_TYPE_ENCRYPTED=1200). The original type is embedded in the first bytes of the plaintext.

files.note: Wire format: magic+type+length+crc32+client_id
Builds header, optionally encrypts, writes to socket
Reads header, validates CRC, decrypts payload

stages.name: Session Data Flow
stages.description: After handshake, the client sends its webcam/file frames as PACKET_TYPE_ASCII_FRAME (compressed ASCII art) or PACKET_TYPE_IMAGE_FRAME_H265 (H.265 encoded video). Audio is sent as PACKET_TYPE_AUDIO_OPUS_BATCH. The server receives frames from all clients, composites them into a grid, and broadcasts the merged ASCII frame back to all clients.

files.note: Server receives ASCII frame from client
Server broadcasts merged frame to all clients
Client receives server frame, dispatches to renderer

notes: WebRTC path (--webrtc): ICE/STUN/TURN negotiation happens via the discovery service. Once the data channel is open, the same acip_transport_t vtable is used — the rest of the code is identical.
Packet compression: packets > 100 KB are zstd-compressed before encryption. The receiver decompresses after decryption.
Rekey: CRYPTO_REKEY_REQUEST/RESPONSE/COMPLETE packets renegotiate the session key without dropping the connection.
PACKET_TYPE_SIZE_MESSAGE (2000) is sent by the server to inform the client of the current terminal grid dimensions, so the client can adjust its local rendering.

flows.title: Discovery Service (ACDS)
flows.subtitle: How two peers find each other without knowing each other's IP address
flows.summary: The ascii-chat Discovery Service (ACDS) is a lightweight signalling server. Servers register a human-readable session string (e.g. 'blue-mountain-tiger'). Clients look up that string and get back connection info. WebRTC ICE candidates are exchanged through the same channel for P2P connections.

stages.name: Session Registration
stages.description: When --discovery is passed, the server sends ACIP_SESSION_CREATE to ACDS with its session string, IP (if --discovery-expose-ip), port, and crypto capabilities. ACDS stores the session in SQLite and returns ACIP_SESSION_CREATED with the canonical session string.

files.note: Sends SESSION_CREATE, stores string in SQLite
Generates or validates the session string
ACDS server: listens for registration/lookup

stages.name: Session Lookup
stages.description: The client sends ACIP_SESSION_LOOKUP with the session string. ACDS returns ACIP_SESSION_INFO with the server's address. If --webrtc, ACDS facilitates SDP and ICE candidate exchange (ACIP_WEBRTC_SDP, ACIP_WEBRTC_ICE) so the peers can establish a direct WebRTC data channel.

files.note: Sends SESSION_LOOKUP, receives SESSION_INFO
WebRTC SDP offer/answer via libdatachannel
UPnP port mapping for NAT traversal

notes: Session strings are human-readable adjective-noun-animal combos (e.g. 'blue-mountain-tiger') for easy verbal sharing.
IP privacy: by default ACDS does not expose the server's IP. --discovery-expose-ip must be explicitly passed.
mDNS (--lan-discovery): on local networks, the server advertises itself via mDNS so clients can auto-discover without ACDS.

flows.title: Session Lifecycle & Startup
flows.subtitle: What happens between typing './ascii-chat client' and seeing the first ASCII frame
flows.summary: Startup is carefully sequenced: logging, colorscheme, terminal capabilities, options parsing, ACDS connection, crypto handshake, splash screen, then the render loop. Each step is guarded by lifecycle_t atomics to prevent TOCTOU races.

stages.name: Early Initialization
stages.description: Before options_init(), the shared subsystem sets up: signal handlers (SIGINT→g_should_exit), early logging to stderr, terminal capability detection (color level, UTF-8 support), colorscheme loading, PCRE2 singleton initialization. The CLAUDECODE env var is checked to enable/disable splash screen and status screen.

files.note: Shared init: signals, logging, terminal, colorscheme
Detects color mode, UTF-8, ANSI support
Sets up log level, grep filter, file output

stages.name: Options Parsing
stages.description: options_init(argc, argv) runs the full parsing pipeline (see Options flow). After this, GET_OPTION() is live.

files.note: Full parse + RCU publish

stages.name: Splash Screen
stages.description: The animated splash screen captures log output (up to 100 entries in a ring buffer) and displays it below a rainbow ASCII art header. It runs in a dedicated thread. Logs during media source creation, FPS probing, and crypto handshake are visible here. The splash exits when the first frame arrives or on timeout.

files.note: Starts splash thread, hooks into log buffer
Called from logging.c for every log line
Server mode: live status screen instead of splash

stages.name: Render Loop
stages.description: session_render_loop() is the main loop: capture frame → apply color filter → convert to ASCII → render to terminal → sleep for FPS. The loop checks g_should_exit (atomic) on every iteration. Keyboard input is polled non-blocking in the same thread. On EOF or SIGINT, the loop exits and session cleanup runs.

files.note: Unified render loop for mirror and client modes
Atomic exit flag, set by signal handler
Calls ascii_convert() with current palette/color mode
Writes ASCII frame to terminal fd

notes: The server mode runs a status screen (not a splash) showing connected clients, frame rate, and the live log feed.
Snapshot mode (--snapshot): the render loop runs exactly one iteration, writes to stdout, then calls _Exit(0). No atexit cleanup (intentional — avoids debug memory report polluting snapshot output).
The lifecycle_t atomic in options_state_init() uses a three-state machine (UNINITIALIZED → INITIALIZING → INITIALIZED) with CAS to prevent double-init races.
